titleStmt.title: SoTCKGE: Continual Knowledge Graph Embedding Based on Spatial Offset Transformation

funder.orgName: National Social Science Foundation of China

affiliation.orgName: School of Cyber Science and Engineering
School of Cyber Science and Engineering
School of Cyber Science and Engineering
School of Cyber Science and Engineering
School of Cyber Science and Engineering
School of Cyber Science and Engineering

analytic.title: SoTCKGE: Continual Knowledge Graph Embedding Based on Spatial Offset Transformation

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Current Continual Knowledge Graph Embedding (CKGE) methods primarily rely on translationbased embedding methods, leveraging previously acquired knowledge to initialize new facts. To enhance learning efficiency, these methods often integrate fine-tuning or continual learning strategies. However, this compromises the model's prediction accuracy and the translation-based methods lack support for complex relational structures (multihop relations). To tackle this challenge, we propose a novel CKGE framework SoTCKGE grounded in Spatial Offset Transformation. Within this framework, entity positions are defined as being jointly determined by base position vectors and offset vectors. This not only enhances the model's ability to represent complex relational structures but also allows for the embedding update of both new and old knowledge through simple spatial offset transformations, without the need for continuous learning methods. Furthermore, we introduce a hierarchical update strategy and a balanced embedding method to refine the parameter update process, effectively minimizing training costs and augmenting model accuracy. To comprehensively assess the performance of our model, we have conducted extensive experimlents on four publicly accessible datasets and a new dataset constructed by us. Experimental results demonstrate the advantage of our model in enhancing multi-hop relationship learning and further improving prediction accuracy.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: The introduction of Knowledge Graph Embedding (KGE) models marked a significant step in representing entities and relationships from multi-relational data in low-dimensional vector spaces. The core idea involves training on known facts to learn embeddings for entities and relationships. These embeddings can then be used to calculate the likelihood scores of all possible facts, providing a robust structural foundation for numerous knowledge-based applications. The TransE model [Bordes et al., 2013] in this domain. It pioneered the evaluation of fact plausibility by translating head entities, relations, and tail entities in vector space. As research advanced, a series of KGE models emerged, offering various optimizations and extensions from different perspectives. However, studies such as [Gutiérrez-Basulto and Schockaert, 2018] revealed that even for the simplest relationship types, translation-based KGE models fail to achieve effective relational modeling. This finding highlights the limitations of existing KGE models and has spurred new research directions. To address these shortcomings, spacetranslation-based KGE methods were introduced [Abboud et al., 2020]. These methods enhance relational modeling by incorporating more complex spatial transformations and relationship representations. Despite these advancements, dynamic knowledge growth has become a critical challenge for large-scale static knowledge graphs. Static KGE models cannot adapt to the demands of dynamic knowledge learning. In response, the Continuous Knowledge Graph Embedding (CKGE) task was introduced, offering new possibilities for constructing and applying dynamic knowledge graphs.

CKGE aims to address the representation of dynamic knowledge by enabling knowledge growth through the learning of new entities and relationships while effectively preserving old knowledge. The challenge is how to efficiently integrate existing knowledge to accurately represent new facts while ensuring seamless alignment between old and new knowledge representations. Current mainstream CKGE methods rely on translation-based embedding techniques, combined with dynamic architectures, memory replay, and regularization, to initialize new entities and facts using previously learned knowledge. While these models improve learning efficiency, they often fail to guarantee predictive accuracy. This limitation arises from their excessive reliance on old knowledge during new knowledge learning and their inability to handle complex relational structures (e.g., MULTI-HOP relationships), as illustrated in Figure 1.

To balance learning efficiency and predictive accuracy while enhancing the representation of complex relational structures, we propose a novel CKGE framework based on region embeddings defined by relationships. Inspired by the embedding model in [Abboud et al., 2020], we define entity positions using both base position vectors and offset vectors. This dual-representation mechanism reduces the dependency of new knowledge on old knowledge, facilitates dynamic interactions between old and new knowledge, and enables the model to better capture logical rules and represent complex relational structures. For new knowledge learning, we design a hierarchical embedding update method. By updating offset vectors with simple operations, we achieve efficient embedding updates for new and old knowledge while minimizing retraining costs. Additionally, to address the long-standing issue of catastrophic forgetting, we introduce an innovative balancing embedding coefficient. This coefficient imposes fine-grained constraints on parameter updates, improving the model's predictive accuracy.

Furthermore, existing CKGE datasets often exhibit a selection bias toward facts directly related to current knowledge, which can lead to overfitting and hinder accurate evaluation of the model's ability to handle MULTI-HOP relationships and new knowledge. To provide a more comprehensive and objective assessment of model performance, we constructed a new dataset, MULTI-HOP. This dataset focuses on incorporating entities with MULTI-HOP relationships, allowing for a deeper investigation of the model's ability to assimilate and integrate new knowledge in continuous learning scenarios. We conducted extensive experiments on four public datasets and our new dataset MULTI-HOP. The results demonstrate that our model outperforms all strong baselines.
The specific contributions of this work are as follows:

• We propose a novel region-based embedding framework, enable the model to capture complex relational structures more effectively. This represents the first attempt to apply space-translation-based methods to the CKGE task. • We define a hierarchical embedding update method, which updates the embeddings of old and new knowledge through simple offset vector operations. This carefully crafted hierarchical mechanism significantly reduces retraining costs. In mainstream continuous knowledge graph embedding (CKGE) methods, only a few adopt embedding techniques beyond TransE. For example, [Hamaguchi et al., 2017] employs graph neural networks (GNNs) to embed new entities and relations, serving as auxiliary knowledge to enhance the model. However, most CKGE methods primarily rely on TransE as their core embedding approach, utilizing fine-tuning or continual learning techniques to update knowledge graphs. Specifically, [Kou et al., 2020] employs a disentangle-based method and designs two independent modules to facilitate knowledge updates. [Cheng et al., 2024] introduces additional parameters via hyper-networks to flexibly update models and improve adaptability. Moreover, [Liu et al., 2024b;Liu et al., 2024a] incorporates incremental distillation mechanisms to hierarchically process new knowledge and employs lightweight incremental adapters with fewer training parameters to reduce the complexity and cost of model updates. [Cui et al., 2023] utilizes a knowledge graph autoencoder based on graph convolutional networks (GCNs) for embedding and learning while applying an embedding transfer strategy to ensure smooth transitions between embeddings. Despite these advancements, these methods, while significantly improving the efficiency of knowledge learning in new snapshots, greatly compromise predictive accuracy. More critically, existing models have a fatal limitation: they struggle to effectively learn new knowledge that lacks direct associations with existing knowledge. Therefore, identifying a balanced embedding strategy that ensures efficiency while improving predictive accuracy remains a pressing challenge in the CKGE domain.

div.head: Preliminary
div.p: Growing Knowledge. Dynamic knowledge graphs are represented as a sequence of evolving snapshots, i.e., G = S 1 , S 2 , . . . , S n . Each snapshot S i is represented as a triplet
, where E, R and T denote the sets of entities, relations, and facts at time i, respectively.
div.formula: ∆E i = E i -E i-1 , ∆R i = R i -R i-1 and ∆T i = T i -T i-1
div.p: are denoted as new entities, relations, and facts. Continual Knowledge Graph Embedding. Continual Knowledge Graph Embedding (CKGE) aims to map newly added entities and relations into the vector space while ensuring a balanced update with old entities and relations. In more details, for each snapshot S i in a sequence of snapshots G , the model learns representations for the newly introduced entities ∆E i and relations ∆R i . These new embeddings are then balanced with the old entity and relation embeddings(E i-1 and R i-1 ), to produce the final representations E i and R i .

div.head: Framework
div.p: The framework of CheeseKGE is depicted in Figure 2. Influenced by previous studies [Abboud et al., 2020;Gutiérrez-Basulto and Schockaert, 2018], we deviate from the traditional approach of low-dimensional vector space embedding for knowledge graphs (KGs). Instead, we adopt a spatialtranslation-based method. At each phase of KG expansion, we learn embeddings for new knowledge and update the embeddings of existing knowledge using offset vectors. Additionally, the degree of interaction between new and existing knowledge is controlled by a balancing coefficient carried over from the prior phase.

div.head: Initialization of Embeddings Based on Spatial Offsets
div.p: Entity Embedding Inspired by [Abboud et al., 2020], we abandon the traditional knowledge graph (KG) embedding methods based on low-dimensional vector spaces and adopt a spatial-translation mechanism for entity embedding. For an entitie e i (e i ∈ E 0 ) in the initial snapshot S 0 , we initialize a base vector ⃗ e bi and an offset vector ⃗ e oi . Our primary objective is to identify and integrate all relationships associated with e i , thereby constructing an entity-relationship set Ω ei , defined as{e j }. In this set, e j represents all entities related to e i through the same established fact. Ultimately, we aim to generate a final entity representation of e i , denoted as ⃗ e i , as shown in Equation (1).
div.formula: ⃗ e i = ⃗ e bi + j∈Ωe i ⃗ e oj(1)
div.p: Relation Embedding In addition to embedding entities, we also need to embed the relationships within the knowledge graph. For each relationship r k , we initialize two learnable tensors, ⃗ r b k and ⃗ r o k , to define the spatial region represented by the relationship. Specifically, for the relationship r k ∈ R 0 in the current snapshot S 0 and its corresponding fact triple (e i , r k , e j ) ∈ T i , where e i is the head entity, r k is the relationship, and e j is the tail entity, we aim to map the head entity e i to the corresponding tail space through the relationship r k . This mapping process can be implemented as follows:

1. Initialize the relationship's base point and offset: For each relationship r, we initialize two learnable tensors: the base center point ⃗ r b k and the offset ⃗ r o k . The tensor ⃗ r b k represents the reference position of the relationship in the space, while ⃗ r o k indicates the extent of the relationship in the space, starting from the base center point. This offset determines the size of the spatial region represented by the relationship.

2. Calculate the boundaries of the relationship's spatial region: Using the base center point ⃗ r b k and the offset ⃗ r o k , we can calculate the upper and lower boundaries of the relationship's spatial region, denoted as l r and h r , respectively. The specific formula for this calculation is
div.formula: provided in Equation (2). l r = min( ⃗ r b k ± 0.5 × ⃗ r o k ) (2) h r = max( ⃗ r b k ± 0.5 × ⃗ r o k )(3)
div.p: Through these steps, we construct a well-defined spatial region for each relationship in the knowledge graph, enabling more accurate capture and representation of the relationship information.

div.head: Hierarchical Embedding Updates
div.p: During the process of exploring new knowledge learning, we propose a hierarchical embedding update strategy. Specifically, for the embedding representations of new entities ∆E i in the i-th snapshot, we adopt a strategically designed initialization scheme.

For novel entities e novel ∈ ∆E i that are completely independent of the existing knowledge system-i.e., entities that do not have direct connections to the outermost layer of the knowledge graph (KG) in the (i -1)-th snapshot and satisfy e novel / ∈ Neighbors(E i-1 ) (where Neighbors(E i-1 ) denotes the set of all direct neighbors of entities in E i-1 )-we assign and initialize a base position vector e novel This approach not only enables the effective initialization of embedding representations for new entities but also dynamically adjusts the embedding representations of old entities based on the integration of new entities. Consequently, it ensures consistency and accuracy in the entity embeddings within the knowledge graph.

div.head: Balancing New and Old Knowledge Embeddings
div.p: After introducing an additional balanced embedding rule, we refined the parameter update process within the framework to impose stricter constraints and achieve a better balance between the integration of new and existing knowledge. To accomplish this, we computed the distance differences between the head and tail entities of new and old facts, relative to their corresponding relationships, and set minimizing these differences as the objective.

Let the head entity, tail entity, and relationship of a new fact be e new head , e new tail , r new , respectively, and those of an old fact be e old head , e old tail , r old . To quantify the embedding differences between new and old knowledge, we define the following metrics:

where dist(•, •) represents a distance computation function that measures the distance between two elements (entities or relationships). This function is specifically designed to account for the associative strength and positional relationships between entities and relationships, as defined below:

where w is a weight parameter, δ(•) is an indicator function that returns a value of 1 if the condition is satisfied and -1 otherwise, and c is the center of the relationship space. Our primary goal is to minimize the defined differences, i.e., to solve min(∆ head , ∆ tail ). To comprehensively capture the differences between head and tail entities in the embeddings of new and old knowledge, we further define an overall difference loss function:
div.formula: L balance = α • ∆ head + β • ∆ tail ,(8)
div.p: where α and β are weighting coefficients used to balance the contributions of the differences in head and tail entities to the loss function. By optimizing this loss function, we aim to minimize the embedding differences between new and old knowledge, thereby achieving a better balance in updating embeddings.

div.head: Overall Objective for Link Predicting
div.p: In the link prediction task, inspired by the core principles of many spatial translation-based embedding methods, we compute scores based on the geometric positions of entity embeddings relative to relation boundaries. This approach avoids the over-reliance on translation-based methods for closely associated facts or those with similar knowledge structures. Specifically, we introduce an innovative loss function, L learn , designed to enhance the model's learning efficiency for distinguishing between positive and negative samples. The calculation is as follows:
div.formula: L learn = -log sigmod(γ -U T ) -∆U • log sigmod(U T -γ) , ∆U = U (t) (t)∈T U (t)(9
div.p: ) where U T and U T represent the overall scores for positive and negative samples, respectively, as defined in Equation ( 10). ∆T serves as the bias weight to balance learning between positive and negative samples, while γ is the margin parameter that regulates the scoring threshold.
div.formula: U (t) = exp dist(head, r) + dist(tail, r)(10)
U T = t∈Tpos U (t), U T = t∈Tneg U (t)(11)
div.p: where U (t) represents the combined score of the head and tail entities for a given fact t. The overall objective function is defined as:
div.formula: ) Dataset Snapshot 0 Snapshot 1 Snapshot 2 Snapshot 3 Snapshot 4 ∆E 0 ∆R 0 ∆T 0 ∆E 1 ∆R 1 ∆T 1 ∆E 2 ∆R 2 ∆T 2 ∆E 3 ∆R 3 ∆T 3 ∆E 4 ∆R 4 ∆T 4 ENTITY 2,

div.head: Experiment Settings
div.p: Baselines. We carefully selected 11 baseline models for comparison, including one non-continual learning method, Finetune [Cui et al., 2023] To ensure a comprehensive and accurate assessment of the model's performance, we adopted seven evaluation metrics. We employed the Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) and Hit@k (with k = 1, 3, 10) to measure the model's accuracy in the link prediction task. We utilized two metrics to evaluate the model's ability of learning new knowledge: Forward Transfer Time (FWT) and Backward Transfer Time (BWT) [Cui et al., 2023]. Finally, to evaluate the efficiency of the model, we also recorded the time taken by the model to process the test set.

Implementation details. All experiments were implemented using PyTorch on an NVIDIA RTX 4060Ti GPU. The time parameter i was selected from the range [0,1,2,3,4], and Adam was used as the optimizer. The learning rate was set to 1 × 10 -4 , chosen from the candidate set [1 × 10 -4 , 5 × 10 -4 , 8 × 10 -4 ] while the batch size was dynamically adjusted within the range [512,1024,2048]. To ensure fairness and reliability, all experimental results were reported as the average of five runs. Moreover, for all control experiments, excluding those involving our proposed model, TransE [Bordes et al., 2013] was used as the baseline KGE model.

div.head: Main Results
div.p: Table 3and Table 4 present experimental results on four publicly available datasets, while Table 5 reports the results on our newly constructed MULTIHOP dataset. Our model demonstrates strong competitive performance across five datasets. Furthermore, the model outperforms all baseline models on the multi-hop dataset, indicating its superior ability to learn multi-hop relationships. It also shows strong competitive performance on the four publicly available single-hop datasets. First, SoTCKGE demonstrated outstanding performance on the multi-hop dataset MULTI-HOP and the publicly available dataset FACT, while also exhibiting strong competitive performance on three additional datasets. Notably, on the MULTI-HOP, SoTCKGE achieved significant improvements in MRR, Hits@1, Hits@3, and Hits@10, with increases of 5.7%, 6.7%, 6.7%, and 7.9%, respectively. This exceptional performance strongly supports the robustness and effectiveness of using relations as region embeddings for multi-hop relationship learning, even in continual learning scenarios.

Moreover, on the publicly available single-hop dataset FACT, SoTCKGE showed performance improvements over the best method, with gains of 0.1%, 4.7%, 0.2%, and 1.5% in MRR, Hits@1, Hits@3, and Hits@10, respectively. Particularly noteworthy is the fact that SoTCKGE outperformed all baseline models in the Hits@1 metric, with improvements Method ENTITY RELATION FACT HYBRID MRR Hits@1 Hits@3 Hits@10 MRR Hits@1 Hits@3 Hits@10 MRR Hits@1 Hits@3 Hits@10 MRR Hits@1 Hits@3 Hits@10 Finetune 0.165 0.085 0.265 0.321 0.093 0.039 0.106 0.

div.head: Method
div.p: ENTITY RELATION MRR Hits@1 Hits@3 Hits@10 MRR Hits@1 Hits@3 Hits@10 SoTCKGE 0. FACT HYBRID MRR Hits@1 Hits@3 Hits@10 MRR Hits@1 Hits@3 Hits@10 SoTCKGE 0.217 0. ranging from 2.1% to 6.7%, further highlighting its higher accuracy and reliability.

Overall, SoTCKGE demonstrated average improvements of 0.14%, 4.44%, 1.94%, and 1.68% in MRR, Hits@1, Hits@3, and Hits@10, respectively, across five datasets compared to the best baseline. While SoTCKGE maintained consistent growth in Hits@1 and Hits@3 on the three additional public datasets, with improvements ranging from 2.1% to 6.1% and 0.2% to 2.0%, respectively, the MRR metric showed a decline on the ENTITY and HYBRID datasets. We hypothesize that this decline may be due to the baseline models using TransE as the underlying knowledge graph embedding (KGE) model, which primarily models relationships based on vector distances between entities. In contrast, SoTCKGE incorporates relation-based region embeddings, which allow for more complex interactions. When faced with ambiguous or polysemous relationships, this difference may cause SoTCKGE to focus more on optimizing the most probable answer positions, rather than uniformly improving the ranking of all relevant answers.

div.head: Ablation Results
div.p: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed hierarchical embedding update strategy and the balanced embedding coefficient, we designed two variants: w/o BEU and w/o EC. Table 5 presents the experimental results for these variants. The results clearly show that removing the EU and BEC leads to a decrease in model performance. Notably, the removal of EU has a particularly significant impact on performance. Specifically, on the five datasets, removing EU results in average decreases of 3.9%, 2.96%, 4.74%, and 3.58% in MRR, Hits@1, Hits@3, and Hits@10, respectively. This phenomenon highlights the crucial role of the hierarchical embedding update strategy in improving model performance, while the balanced embedding coefficient also positively impacts performance, albeit to a lesser extent.

div.head: MULTI-HOP Prediction Capability
div.p: As shown, on the MULTI-HOP dataset, SoTCKGE outperforms all baseline models across four evaluation metrics. To assess the model's ability to predict multi-hop relationships, we considered the characteristics of the dataset construction. In the first four snapshots, the ratio of single-hop to multihop relationships is relatively balanced, so the model trained on the fifth snapshot was evaluated using the Hits@1 score on the test sets of the first four snapshots. The number of facts in the test sets for the first four snapshots are 7666(67), 7018(60), 16441(164), and 16417(151), with the numbers in parentheses representing the multi-hop relationships. The results, shown in Figure 3, demonstrate that SoTCKGE exhibits stronger capabilities in handling multi-hop facts compared to the other baseline models.
div.formula: S o T C K G E I n c D E F a s t K G E L K G E E W C S I D i C G R L E M R G E M C W R P N N F i n e t u n e 0.0 0.1 0.2 A Multi-hop snapshot0 snapshot1 snapshot2 snapshot3 S o T C K G E I n c D E F a s t K G E L K G E E W C S I D i C G R L E M R G E M C W R P N N F i
div.p: Learning Performance Over Time

The last figure in Figure 4 illustrates the dynamic learning capability of our model over time. Specifically, in Model 3 and Model 4, as time progresses and knowledge accumulates, the model snapshots at different time points are able to maintain stable learning performance. This phenomenon clearly demonstrates the ability of SoTCKGE to balance the integration of new and old knowledge, effectively absorbing new information while preserving existing knowledge, thus ensuring a smooth fusion and embedding of both.The comparison results of FWT and BWT across different models and datasets demonstrate that, on the multi-hop dataset MULTI-HOP, SoTCKGE outperforms all baseline models in knowledge transfer ability. Moreover, it remains highly competitive on the other four public datasets.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: In this paper, we propose a novel CKGE framework based on relation-based regional embeddings. In this framework, the position of an entity is jointly controlled by a base position vector and an offset vector. This design not only significantly enhances the model's ability to represent complex relational structures, but also enables efficient embedding updates of both new and old knowledge through simple spatial transformations, without relying on continuous learning methods. Additionally, we introduce a hierarchical update strategy and a balanced embedding coefficient to refine the parameter update process. This approach not only reduces training costs but also improves model accuracy. Future research will further explore the integration of the relationbased regional CKGE framework with continuous learning methods to enhance both learning efficiency and accuracy.

figure.head: Figure 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 1: Limitations of mainstream CKGE frameworks. The top section demonstrates how current methods, such as TransE, fail to represent complex relational structures. The bottom section illustrates the reliance of mainstream CKGE training frameworks on old knowledge for representing new information.

figure.head: b
figure.figDesc: and an offset vector e novel o . For directly connected entities e direct ∈ ∆E i that satisfy e direct ∈ Neighbors(E i-1 ), we adopt a more streamlined initialization approach. After initializing the base position vector e direct b and the offset vector e direct o for the new entity e direct , we simultaneously update the offset vectors e o of all old entities {e} ⊆ E i-1 directly connected to e direct as follows: e o ← e direct o + δ(e direct o , e), ∀e ∈ Neighbors(e direct ) ∩ E i-1 (4) where δ(e direct o , e) denotes the update function for adjusting the offset vector e o of the old entity e based on the offset vector e direct o of the new entity e direct and their relationship.

figure.head: Figure 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 3: Results represent the probability distribution for multihop and single-hop relationships, when model achieves the Hits@1 accuracy on the test sets of the four snapshots in the MULTI-HOP dataset.

figure.head: Figure 4 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 4: Results of SoTCKGE's dynamic learning capability at each time. Model i represents the model trained on the i-th snapshot.

figure.head: Figure 5 :Figure 6 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 5: Effectiveness of different methods for embedding update.
is an early and notable representative

figure.head: Table 1 :
figure.figDesc: Statistics of four public datasets across different snapshots. ∆Ei, ∆Ri and ∆Ti denote the sets of cumulative entities, relations and facts in the i-th snapshot, respectively. However, in real-world scenarios, new knowledge may also emerge that is not directly linked to the current knowledge graph, such as multi-hop knowledge. To address this, we extended the ENTITY dataset to create a new multi-hop CKGE dataset, following the principle of adding one multihop entity for every five single-hop entities, until no further multi-hop entities could be added at a given time point. Table 1 provides detailed statistics for all datasets. Each dataset contains five time snapshots, with the training, validation, and test sets split in a 3:1:1 ratio. Additionally, Table2summarizes the number of multi-hop and single-hop facts at different snapshots in the MULTI-HOP dataset.

row.cell: We selected five datasets for our experiments: ENTITY, RE-
LATION, FACT, HYBRID, and MULTI-HOP. The first four
are publicly available CKGE datasets, while the latter is a
newly constructed dataset. In the construction of the new
snapshots for ENTITY, RELATION, FACT, and HYBRID,
we primarily added new knowledge related to existing enti-

figure.head: Table 3 :
figure.figDesc: Experimental results obtained on the test sets across all snapshots of ENTITY, RELATION, FACT. The bolded results represent the best performance, while the underlined results indicate the second-best performance.

figure.head: Table 4 :
figure.figDesc: Experimental results obtained on the test sets across all snapshots of HYBRID and MULTI-HOP.

figure.head: Table 5 :
figure.figDesc: Ablation results on ENTITY, RELATION, FACT, HYBRID and MULTI-HOP.

div.head: Acknowledgments
div.p: This work was supported by the China National Key R&D Plan [No. 2020YFA0607902 and No. 2023YFB3106900], the National Science Foundation of China [No. 62372334] and the National Social Science Foundation of China [No. 22&ZD326].
